topic: Hidden concurrency risks of row-level locking with Foreign Keys
shortDescription: Every insert into a child table acquires a FOR SHARE lock on the referenced parent row. In high-traffic systems this creates a hot-row bottleneck where thousands of transactions compete for the same parent record, leading to deadlocks and throughput ceilings.

topic: Foreign Key validation latency and write overhead
shortDescription: Each write to a child table forces a mandatory index lookup on the parent table to verify referential integrity. The cumulative CPU and I/O cost across millions of rows becomes a significant bottleneck in high-throughput environments like logging or real-time event processing.

To avoid full-table scans on parent deletes, child tables must be manually indexed on the FK column. These extra indexes grow storage and slow every write because the DB must update multiple B-Tree structures simultaneously.

topic: Primary and Foreign Key performance trade-offs at scale
shortDescription: Three critical overheads: (1) missing FK indexes cause sequential scans on parent deletes; (2) referential checks create FOR SHARE lock contention on hot parent rows; (3) natural keys increase write amplification compared to surrogate keys as large mutable strings must cascade across all referencing tables.

topic: JSONB vs. standard relational schema — when and trade-offs
shortDescription: Use JSONB for sparse or dynamic data (e-commerce attributes, external API responses, user-defined forms) where the schema changes too often for migrations. Trade-offs: you lose column statistics (poor query plans), GIN indexes are larger and slower to update than B-Tree, and you lose type safety and FK enforcement.

PostgreSQL stores multiple versions of each row directly in the main table. An UPDATE marks the old row as dead and inserts a new one. This means readers never block writers and writers never block readers.
longDescription: Unlike Oracle's undo segments, Postgres keeps old row versions (dead tuples) in the table itself. Each row carries xmin and xmax transaction IDs; a row is visible to a transaction if its xmin is committed before the transaction started and its xmax is not yet committed. Dead tuples accumulate until VACUUM reclaims them.

shortDescription: MVCC leaves dead tuples in the table. The VACUUM process reclaims that space. If autovacuum cannot keep up, table bloat grows — disk usage increases and query performance degrades even when row counts stay stable.
longDescription: VACUUM marks dead tuple space as reusable but does not return it to the OS; VACUUM FULL rewrites the table but holds an exclusive lock. Autovacuum runs automatically based on thresholds (autovacuum_vacuum_threshold + autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor × table size). Tuning these per-table is critical for write-heavy workloads.

topic: Indexing: B-Tree vs. GIN vs. BRIN
shortDescription: B-Tree is the default for equality and range queries. GIN is required for JSONB containment and full-text search. BRIN is extremely small and fast to build for massive time-ordered tables — it stores only the min/max per block range, so it only works well on naturally ordered data.

JSON stores a verbatim text copy (fast writes, slow reads, no indexing). JSONB stores a decomposed binary format (slightly slower writes, much faster reads, supports GIN indexing). Always use JSONB in production.

CTEs (WITH clause) break complex queries into readable named steps. Window functions (ROW_NUMBER, RANK, LAG, LEAD) compute values across a set of rows related to the current row without collapsing them into groups like GROUP BY does.

PostgreSQL spawns a new OS process per connection, which is expensive. In a high-traffic Node.js app, thousands of connections exhaust server memory. Use a pool (pg-pool in the app, or PgBouncer externally) to reuse a small set of persistent connections.

topic: Transaction isolation levels and row locking
shortDescription: Default is Read Committed. Use SELECT ... FOR UPDATE to lock rows during a read-modify-write cycle and prevent race conditions. Repeatable Read prevents phantom reads within a transaction. Serializable provides full serializability at the cost of potential rollbacks.

Changes are written to the WAL log before the actual data files are modified. This guarantees crash recovery, powers streaming replication for high availability, and enables point-in-time recovery (PITR).

Declarative partitioning (Postgres 10+) splits a large table into smaller child tables by range, list, or hash. The query planner prunes irrelevant partitions, and maintenance operations like VACUUM run faster on smaller chunks.

Avoid ORM auto-sync features in production. Use version-controlled SQL migration files (Knex, TypeORM, Flyway, Liquibase). This ensures schema changes are auditable, reproducible, and identical across dev, staging, and production.
